question: In Arkansas, how far before a turn must you begin signaling?
choices: At least 150 feet before you start the turn
At least 100 feet
At least 200 feet ahead of the turn
At least 300 feet before turning
explanation: Arkansas law requires a continuous turn signal during at least the last 100 feet before turning. Signal earlier at highway speeds.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 33, 53 — Signaling to Change Directions

question: When making a right turn, you should finish the turn in:
choices: The lane closest to the curb (right lane)
Whichever through lane is open
The center lane first, then ease over to the right
The left lane first, then move all the way over to the right
explanation: Turn right from the right lane into the right lane. Cutting across to a far lane during a turn is illegal and dangerous.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 33 — Right Turns

question: A two-way center turn lane (shared left-turn lane) may be used for:
choices: Passing slow traffic
Through travel when lanes are heavy
Parking there a while as you run a quick errand inside a store
Making left turns (and waiting to merge), only
explanation: The center turn lane is only for starting and completing left turns from either direction — never for passing or through travel.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 32 — Shared Center Lane

question: How close to a fire hydrant may you legally park?
choices: No closer than 10 feet from it
No closer than 25 feet to it
No closer than 15 feet
No closer than 40 feet
explanation: Parking is prohibited within 15 feet of a fire hydrant so fire crews always have access.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 42 — Do Not Park

question: Parking is NOT allowed within ____ of a stop sign, yield sign, or traffic signal.
choices: 10 ft
30 feet
25 ft
5 ft
explanation: Stay at least 30 feet back from stop signs, yield signs, and signals so other drivers can see them. Also: 20 ft from a crosswalk, 50 ft from a railroad crossing.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 42 — Do Not Park

question: When parking downhill WITH a curb, turn your front wheels:
choices: Toward the curb
Out from curb
Straight
Any way if the brake is firmly set
explanation: Downhill: wheels toward the curb. Uphill with a curb: wheels away from the curb (so the car rolls back into it). No curb: wheels toward the edge of the road. Always set the parking brake.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 41 — Parking Tips (Hills)

question: Before backing up, the most important thing to do is:
choices: Glance at your rearview mirror only
Trust the backup camera by itself to show all that is behind you
Sound your horn twice and then slowly back up to warn anyone who may be nearby
Turn and look over your right shoulder through the rear window
explanation: Mirrors and cameras have blind zones. Physically turn your head and look through the rear window while backing slowly.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 41 — Parking (Backing)

question: When turning left at an intersection, you should avoid cutting the corner because:
choices: It wears your front tires out a bit faster than usual
It is only a real concern for the drivers of large trucks and buses, so ordinary car drivers never need to worry at all about cutting the corner on any left turn they make
Turning sharp could cause you to hit a vehicle approaching from the left — and always leave room for oncoming vehicles turning left in front of you
It can confuse your car's navigation system and cause it to lose your position for a moment, making it briefly recalculate the whole route as you complete the left turn ahead
explanation: Don't cut left-turn corners: a sharp early turn puts you into the path of traffic approaching from the left. Begin signaling and slowing at least 100 feet from the corner, and leave room for oncoming left-turners.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 33 — Left Turns

question: When parallel parking, your vehicle should be no more than ____ from the curb.
choices: 6 in
18 inches
12 inches from the curb
30 inches
explanation: Park within 18 inches of the curb — parking farther out than 18 inches is on the Do-Not-Park list.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 41-42 — Parking Tips / Do Not Park

question: Before leaving a parked car on a hill, Arkansas requires you to:
choices: Leave the transmission in neutral so it can roll if bumped
Leave the engine running with the heater on the entire time the car sits parked
Park on flat, level ground, since a slope usually is not safe to park on
Set the parking brake AND turn the wheels to the curb or side of the highway
explanation: No driver may leave a vehicle unattended on a grade without setting the parking brake and turning the wheels to the curb or side of the highway. Also leave it in 'park' (or in gear with a manual).
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 41 — Parking Tips

question: Which of these is on Arkansas's DO-NOT-PARK list?
choices: On a bridge, overpass, tunnel, or underpass
Anywhere within 100 feet of any roadway intersection
On a public street after dark
Within 5 feet of a residential driveway
explanation: Never park on bridges, overpasses, or in tunnels/underpasses — or in intersections, on railroad tracks, beside another parked car (double parking), or blocking driveways or lowered curbs.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 42 — Do Not Park

question: Parking alongside another vehicle that is parked at the curb (double parking) is:
choices: Permitted
Prohibited
Legal
Allowed
explanation: Double parking is on the Do-Not-Park list — it blocks a travel lane and traps the parked car.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 42 — Do Not Park

question: You plan to turn into a driveway just BEYOND an intersection. When should you signal?
choices: At least 100 feet before the intersection
Not at all; driveways usually don't call for a signal
Just after you pass through the intersection — signaling earlier could make drivers think you're turning at the intersection
Right as you begin the turn into the driveway
explanation: Signal just after passing through the intersection. An early signal misleads other drivers — including anyone waiting to pull out between you and your turn.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 53 — Signaling to Change Directions

question: After you complete a turn or lane change, you should:
choices: Leave it on, set for your next turn
Flash your hazards once to thank drivers
Tap the brake lightly to alert the car behind
Turn your signal off — a false turn signal is unsafe
explanation: Cancel your signal after every turn or lane change. A signal left on tells other drivers a turn is coming that isn't — and they may pull out in front of you.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 53 — Signaling to Change Directions

question: Why must you look over your shoulder before changing lanes?
choices: Your rear-view and side mirrors cannot show vehicles in your blind spots — the rear corners of your vehicle
To make sure your seat belt is buckled snugly across your lap and shoulder before you begin changing lanes for safety
Because turning your head is required only for the drivers of large trucks, buses, and other oversized commercial vehicles
To make eye contact with the driver behind you so that they clearly understand you are about to change lanes ahead
explanation: Blind spots are the rear corners your mirrors can't see. Check mirrors, then turn your head for a quick glance over your shoulder — but don't take your eyes off the road ahead for more than an instant.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 49-50 — Changing Lanes / Blind Spots

question: Before backing out of a parking space, when should you check behind your vehicle?
choices: Right after you start the engine, using your mirrors to scan the area behind the car for any hazards
BEFORE you get in — children and small objects cannot be seen from the driver's seat
Only when parked in a school or daycare lot
Never; a backup camera shows it all
explanation: Walk-around first: check behind the vehicle before you get in. Then back slowly with your body turned, looking directly through the rear window — and use a helper outside whenever possible.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 50 — Backing

question: When backing up, you should:
choices: Back up fast to spend the least time in reverse
Rely only on the mirror to watch behind
Back slowly — your vehicle is much harder to steer in reverse
Keep both hands at the 10 and 2 o'clock positions
explanation: Back slowly. Place your right arm over the seat back, turn your body, and look directly through the rear window — mirrors can't show what's directly behind you.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 50 — Backing

question: You miss your turn on a busy street. You should:
choices: Stop and carefully back up to the turn you missed
Make a quick U-turn directly from your current lane to get yourself back to the turn that you missed
Stop right where you are in your lane and wait for a gap so you can safely reverse to the missed turn
Continue to a safe place to turn around — backing in a travel lane is illegal and unsafe
explanation: Never back up in any travel lane, and never stop in a travel lane for any reason — keep moving until you can safely pull off and turn around.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 32 — General Driving

question: Before slowing down — for example, to turn into a driveway — you should:
choices: Check the traffic behind you in your mirrors and allow plenty of time to slow gradually
Brake firmly right away to scrub off all of your speed just as fast as you possibly can right then and there
Switch on your hazard lights to warn drivers behind
Swerve toward the right edge of the road, then stop
explanation: Check behind before slowing. Gradual, signaled slowing keeps the driver behind you from being surprised — especially where they wouldn't expect a stop, like a private driveway.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 50 — Slowing

question: You want to parallel park but a car is following close behind you. You should:
choices: Turn on your signal, pull up next to the space, and let traffic behind you pass before backing in
Stop short so the driver behind is forced to go around you
Skip the space and circle the block until no one is following close
Wave the driver behind you to go around while you start backing into the space
explanation: Signal early, pull alongside the space, and let following traffic clear before you back in. Helping the driver behind you know your intentions prevents rear-end crashes.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 60 — The Safety Cushion (Parallel Parking)

question: Which is the correct way to grip the steering wheel for a sharp turn, such as around a corner?
choices: Palm the wheel with one hand and spin it around the corner
Grip the very bottom of the wheel with both hands
Steer using only your knee for a moment so that one hand stays free to operate the turn signal
Use the hand-over-hand technique, with hands on opposite sides of the wheel
explanation: Keep hands on opposite sides of the wheel and use hand-over-hand for sharp turns — never turn the wheel in the palm of your hand. Return the wheel to track the direction of travel after the turn.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 44 — Steering the Vehicle

question: When exiting your parked car on a street, Arkansas's guide says you should:
choices: Open the door quickly and step out to claim the space before another driver can take it from you
Usually exit on the street side of the car, regardless of which way the traffic happens to be flowing past
Check traffic before opening the door, and exit on the curb side whenever possible
Leave the door standing wide open the whole time you are loading your items into the parked car
explanation: Check traffic before opening your door, and use the curb side when you can. If you must exit street-side, look again before stepping out.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 41 — Parking Tips

question: On a road with three or more lanes traveling in the same direction, you should generally:
choices: Stay in the far-left lane and use it as your normal cruising lane the whole trip
Remain in the right lane except to pass (use the center lane if there is a lot of merging traffic)
Use whichever lane happens to have the fewest cars right now
Change lanes constantly, weaving between them to keep moving fast
explanation: On three-or-more same-direction lanes, stay in the right lane except to pass. If there is a lot of merging traffic, use the center travel lane. The far-left lane is for passing.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 32 — General Driving

question: Driving on the shoulder of a street or road is:
choices: Never allowed unless a police officer directs you to
Perfectly fine any time you need to get around slow traffic ahead of you
Allowed when your hazards are on
Allowed if it is paved
explanation: Never drive on the shoulder unless directed by a police officer, and never pass on the shoulder (paved or not) — other drivers won't expect you there and may pull off without looking.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 32 — General Driving / Passing

question: On a multilane highway, when you find yourself driving right beside another vehicle, you should:
choices: Stay exactly even with it so you can watch what the driver does
Speed up past the limit to clear past it quickly
Match its speed and ride alongside it the whole way
Move ahead of it or drop behind it, rather than riding alongside in its blind spot
explanation: Don't ride alongside another vehicle on a multilane road — move ahead or drop back so you're not stuck in each other's blind spot. If someone follows you too closely, move into the lane to your right when clear.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 34 — Multiple Lanes

question: Where signs are posted, Arkansas's Do-Not-Park list prohibits parking within how many feet of a fire-station driveway (on the same side of the street)?
choices: 5 ft
100 feet
20 feet
1,000 feet
explanation: Don't park within 20 feet of a fire-station driveway on the same side of the street where signs are posted, so fire apparatus can get out quickly.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 42 — Do Not Park

question: A curb painted YELLOW means:
choices: A fire zone — no stopping allowed
A loading zone or other parking restriction
Free parking, with no time limit or restriction at all
A space reserved only for disabled-permit holders
explanation: A yellow curb marks a loading zone or other restriction. (A red curb means a fire zone.) Colored curb markings indicate restricted parking areas.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 42 — Restricted Parking Zones

question: Turn signals should be used before:
choices: A lane change, a left or right turn, and each time you merge into traffic or park
A left turn, though right turns and lane changes usually need no signal first
The turns you make at intersections, though driveways and alleys typically need none
Mainly when there are other cars nearby that could be affected by the move you make
explanation: Signal before every lane change, every left or right turn, and each time you merge into traffic or park. Proper signaling gives other drivers time to react to your moves.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 53 — Signaling to Change Directions

question: When speeding up from a stop, you should:
choices: Accelerate just as hard as you possibly can so that you reach the speed of the surrounding traffic in the very shortest time that you are able to
Spin the tires briefly to break them free of the pavement and get the heavy vehicle moving away from the stop more quickly
Rev the engine up first to build up plenty of power and then release the brake to launch yourself smoothly away from the stop
Accelerate smoothly and gradually — sudden acceleration is harder to control and can cause a skid on a slippery road
explanation: Accelerate smoothly and gradually. Hard, sudden acceleration makes the vehicle harder to control and can break traction (spin or skid) on wet or icy surfaces.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 44 — Acceleration

question: To keep from unintentionally speeding, the study guide suggests you:
choices: Rely on how fast the car feels and adjust by that feeling alone
Glance at your speedometer from time to time — it is easy to go faster than you think
Watch the other cars and just keep pace with the flow
Use cruise control on every road so it holds your speed
explanation: Monitor your speed by checking the speedometer periodically. It's easy to drive faster than you realize, especially after leaving a high-speed road.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 44 — Monitor Vehicle Speed

question: Which is also on Arkansas's Do-Not-Park list?
choices: In any legal, marked parking space that is located in the downtown area of a busy city
Along a quiet residential street overnight while the residents who live there are all asleep
On a crosswalk or sidewalk in a construction area if your vehicle blocks traffic
Inside a public parking garage on any of the levels that happen to be above the very ground floor
explanation: Don't park on a crosswalk or sidewalk in a construction area if your vehicle blocks traffic. Other no-park spots include intersections, within 20 ft of a crosswalk, and blocking a curb ramp.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 42 — Do Not Park
